title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a conservation officer called Mia talking about a project protecting coastal sand dunes. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a conservation officer called Mia talking about a project protecting coastal sand dunes. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A conservation officer called Mia describes a project protecting coastal sand dunes.
extracts.audio_script: Hello everyone. I’m Mia from CoastCare, a local organisation that protects the natural coastline. I’m here to explain our project at West Sands, a beach popular with residents and visitors. The wide area behind the beach may look entirely natural now, but until around twenty years ago part of it was used as a [9]camping site[/9]. When that closed, the council allowed sand dunes and coastal plants to recover. Our project works to prevent damage and help people enjoy the beach responsibly.||The dunes are more important than simply providing a beautiful view. Their plants hold sand in place and reduce the effect of storms. We also monitor wildlife living there. People expect us to count insects or small mammals, but our main concern during the spring is protecting [10]nesting birds[/10], whose eggs can be difficult to notice on the ground. New volunteers attend a short beach walk before taking on tasks. The first thing they learn is not plant names but how to follow the marked [11]safe paths[/11] without stepping into sensitive areas.||Our practical sessions usually begin in the morning, before the beach becomes busy. Volunteers may think they will spend the whole time picking up rubbish. Clean-up is part of the work, and everyone is supplied with [12]litter bags[/12] and protective equipment, but we also repair signs and make observations. One important activity is replacing the low [13]wooden fences[/13] that guide walkers away from new dunes. These are deliberately simple and natural-looking, but they make a real difference when thousands of people visit during summer. Volunteers work in teams and always follow instructions from a project leader.||Education is equally important. Last year we placed temporary posters near the car park, but wind and rain quickly damaged them. This season, information about plants and wildlife will be shown on a new [14]visitor board[/14] at the entrance to the beach. We also collect seeds for future planting. The plant used most frequently to strengthen newly forming dunes is [15]marram grass[/15], whose long roots help to trap sand. Our extra training event last summer was about bird watching. This year most volunteers have registered for a [16]photography exhibition[/16], where a local photographer will explain how coastal images can encourage conservation without leading people into protected areas.||Volunteers attending four practical sessions are given a lightweight [17]waterproof jacket[/17] with the CoastCare name, which is useful on windy beaches and makes team members easy to identify. Finally, fundraising this year will support visitors as well as nature. The path from the car park becomes very difficult for wheelchairs and pushchairs. Money from our August beach event will pay for an [18]accessible path[/18] to the viewing area, designed so the dunes are still protected. You can sign up online or complete one of the forms by the door after this talk.

dialogue.speaker: mia
dialogue.text: Hello everyone. I’m Mia from CoastCare, a local organisation that protects the natural coastline. I’m here to explain our project at West Sands, a beach popular with residents and visitors. The wide area behind the beach may look entirely natural now, but until around twenty years ago part of it was used as a camping site. When that closed, the council allowed sand dunes and coastal plants to recover. Our project works to prevent damage and help people enjoy the beach responsibly.||The dunes are more important than simply providing a beautiful view. Their plants hold sand in place and reduce the effect of storms. We also monitor wildlife living there. People expect us to count insects or small mammals, but our main concern during the spring is protecting nesting birds, whose eggs can be difficult to notice on the ground. New volunteers attend a short beach walk before taking on tasks. The first thing they learn is not plant names but how to follow the marked safe paths without stepping into sensitive areas.||Our practical sessions usually begin in the morning, before the beach becomes busy. Volunteers may think they will spend the whole time picking up rubbish. Clean-up is part of the work, and everyone is supplied with litter bags and protective equipment, but we also repair signs and make observations. One important activity is replacing the low wooden fences that guide walkers away from new dunes. These are deliberately simple and natural-looking, but they make a real difference when thousands of people visit during summer. Volunteers work in teams and always follow instructions from a project leader.||Education is equally important. Last year we placed temporary posters near the car park, but wind and rain quickly damaged them. This season, information about plants and wildlife will be shown on a new visitor board at the entrance to the beach. We also collect seeds for future planting. The plant used most frequently to strengthen newly forming dunes is marram grass, whose long roots help to trap sand. Our extra training event last summer was about bird watching. This year most volunteers have registered for a photography exhibition, where a local photographer will explain how coastal images can encourage conservation without leading people into protected areas.||Volunteers attending four practical sessions are given a lightweight waterproof jacket with the CoastCare name, which is useful on windy beaches and makes team members easy to identify. Finally, fundraising this year will support visitors as well as nature. The path from the car park becomes very difficult for wheelchairs and pushchairs. Money from our August beach event will pay for an accessible path to the viewing area, designed so the dunes are still protected. You can sign up online or complete one of the forms by the door after this talk.
